coredata.dc:title: Loquat fruit-based carbonquantumdots as an “ON-OFF” probe for fluorescent assay of MnO4 − in waters based on the joint action of inner filter effect and static quenching
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Panpan
Wang, Junxia
Liu, Tingting
Sun, Zhengpeng
Gao, Ming
Huang, Kai
Wang, Xuedong
coredata.dc:description: As one kind of high nutrition fruits, abandoned loquat causes great waste due to short storage period. For resource utilization, we herein fabricated the loquat fruit-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by a facile/green hydrothermal method. The as-fabricated CQDs showed spherical shape with an average size of 4.46 nm, and richness in functional hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties on their surface. Under daylight and UV light, the as-fabricated CQDs displayed light yellow and blue color, respectively, with a fluorescence quantum yield of 10.36%. Upon the addition of MnO4 −, the fluorescence intensity of loquat-based CQDs was prominently quenched in a concentration-dependent manner, suggesting the feasibility for constructing a sensitive “ON-OFF” nanoprobe. By virtue of changes in UV-Vis absorption, fluorescence lifetime, Stern-Volmer constant and zeta potential, the MnO4 −-triggered quenching mechanisms were attributable to the joint action of inner filter effect and static quenching. Under optimized conditions, the CQDs supplied wide linear range of 0.2–150 μM, low detection limit of 0.06 μM, and high recovery of 96.0–106.0% for MnO4 − in environmental waters. Especially, it exhibited the superior selectivity and anti-inference capacity for other commonly existed cations and anions in waters. In contrast to China authentic indirect iodometry method and previously reported approaches, the newly developed “ON-OFF” nanoprobe possesses comparable or even better analytical performance. As far as our information goes, it is the first report on the loquat fruit-based CQDs for successful application in the assay of trace-level MnO4 − in environmental waters, thereby showing great potential in conventional monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Loquat fruit
Carbon quantum dots
“ON-OFF” nanoprobe
MnO4 − ion
Inner filter effect and static quenching

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent CQDs from loquat fruits
Detection of MnO4 − in real-world water samples
Experimental optimization for the assay of MnO4 −
Sensitive and selective detection of MnO4 − by the loquat fruit-based CQDs
Quenching mechanisms of CQDs by MnO4 −

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was jointly sponsored by the China National Natural Science Foundation (21876125 and 22076134), Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Science and Engineering (Zd1802), and Suzhou City Science and Technology Project (SNG2018050).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of CQDs (inset: the corresponding lattice spacing) (a); particle size distribution histograms of CQDs (b); XRD pattern of CQDs (c); and FT-IR spectra of CQDs (d).
XPS full survey spectra of CQDs (a); High-resolution XPS spectra of C1s of CQDs (b); High-resolution XPS spectra of O1s of CQDs (c); High-resolution XPS spectra of N1s of CQDs (d).

Fluorescence spectra and UV-Vis absorption of CQDs (inset: photographs of CQDs under daylight and UV light) (a); Emission spectra of CQDs at varying excitation wavelengths (b); 3D topographical contour map of CQDs (c); The chromaticity coordinates corresponding to the maximum excitation intensity and the maximum emission intensity (λex/λem = 350 nm/440 nm) of CQDs chromaticity coordinates map (d).

Fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of CQDs and the UV-Vis absorption of MnO4 − (a); UV-vis absorption spectra of CQDs, MnO4 −1 solution, and the mixed solution of CQDs and MnO4 −1 (b); Zeta potential (c); and The fluorescence decay curves of CQDs solution before and after the addition of MnO4 −1(λex = 350 nm, λem = 440 nm, C MnO4-=100 μM) (d).
Schematic diagram of the loquat-based CQDs for the detection of MnO4 −.
Detection of MnO4 − by the loquat-based CQDs in real-world waters.
The analytical performance for MnO4 − detection by different methods.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Notes: PNI-MSPC: N-isopropylacrylamide (NIPAm), 1,1′-di[4-(vinyl)benzyl]-4,4′-pyridinium salt (MSPC2+·2Cl−). CdL: [Cd2L(Hpmad)2(H2O)2]·3H2O. Refs.: references.

head.ce:title: Loquat fruit-based carbonquantumdots as an “ON-OFF” probe for fluorescent assay of MnO4 − in waters based on the joint action of inner filter effect and static quenching

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Science and Engineering, School of Environmental Science and Engineering, Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou 215009, China

School of Environmental Science and Engineering
Suzhou University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Shanghai Xin Jinqiao Environmental Protection Company, 870 Jingye Road, Shanghai 201200, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shanghai Xin Jinqiao Environmental Protection Company

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Shanghai Xin Jinqiao Environmental Protection Company, 870 Jingye Road, Shanghai 201200, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As one kind of high nutrition fruits, abandoned loquat causes great waste due to short storage period. For resource utilization, we herein fabricated the loquat fruit-based carbon quantum dots (CQDs) by a facile/green hydrothermal method. The as-fabricated CQDs showed spherical shape with an average size of 4.46 nm, and richness in functional hydroxyl and carboxyl moieties on their surface. Under daylight and UV light, the as-fabricated CQDs displayed light yellow and blue color, respectively, with a fluorescence quantum yield of 10.36%. Upon the addition of MnO4 −, the fluorescence intensity of loquat-based CQDs was prominently quenched in a concentration-dependent manner, suggesting the feasibility for constructing a sensitive “ON-OFF” nanoprobe. By virtue of changes in UV-Vis absorption, fluorescence lifetime, Stern-Volmer constant and zeta potential, the MnO4 −-triggered quenching mechanisms were attributable to the joint action of inner filter effect and static quenching. Under optimized conditions, the CQDs supplied wide linear range of 0.2–150 μM, low detection limit of 0.06 μM, and high recovery of 96.0–106.0% for MnO4 − in environmental waters. Especially, it exhibited the superior selectivity and anti-inference capacity for other commonly existed cations and anions in waters. In contrast to China authentic indirect iodometry method and previously reported approaches, the newly developed “ON-OFF” nanoprobe possesses comparable or even better analytical performance. As far as our information goes, it is the first report on the loquat fruit-based CQDs for successful application in the assay of trace-level MnO4 − in environmental waters, thereby showing great potential in conventional monitoring.

ce:keyword.ce:text: Inner filter effect and static quenching

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) that were pioneered in 2004 are nanoparticles with diameter less than 10 nm [1]. Compared to traditional semiconductor quantum dots, they have superior luminescence properties, good biocompatibility, high chemical stability and lower toxicity, which have activated great interest worldwide [2]. In recent past, CQDs have been extensively used in the detection of metal ions [3], small organic molecules [4–5], biological imaging [6], and photocatalytic degradation [7]. The synthetic methods of CQDs are generally divided into two categories: the top-down and bottom-up approaches. Top-down approach refers to stripping small-sized carbon dots from large-sized carbon sources by physical and chemical means [8], which has many disadvantages, such as high requirements for equipment, low quantum yield, and low purity [9]. In stark contrast, bottom-up approach, especially for the hydrothermal synthesis, is sustainable green and efficient with simple operation, mild reaction conditions and high quantum yield [10]. Among which, natural carbon sources are the predominant precursors for the preparation of CQDs owing to the distinct advantages of green, economy and reuse of abandoned plants [11]. For example, CQDs were fabricated based on various natural biomass sources, such as coconut [12], aloe [13], Carica papaya waste pulp [14], ginkgo leaf [15], and orange peels [16].

As an evergreen fruit tree of the Rosaceae family, loquat (Eriobotrya japonica (Thunb.) Lindl) is native to the subtropical regions of China [17], which is popular for its unique flavor and high nutrition value, as well as dual functions of medicine and food. However, the loquat fruit is perishable after being picked, and thus the shorter storage time reduces its commercial value [18]. Therefore, there is a crucial requirement for extending the storage time after harvesting or to reutilize it as a biological resource. Loquat is an excellent biomass material for the preparation of CQDs; however, the fluorescence characteristics for loquat-based CQDs and the fluorescence differences from other biomass CQDs (such as Purple perilla [19], bamboo leaf [20], and Rose-Heart Radish [21]) or different loquat species have not been involved up to now. Worth noting, the CQDs, prepared by loquat fruits from different regions, climatic environment and soil conditions, may have varying fluorescence properties. For the reuse of abandoned loquat, we herein attempted to employ the loquat fruit to prepare CQDs, which possibly displayed the feasibility for its abundant soluble solids, titratable acids, carotenoids, and polyphenols.

Manganese (Mn) is an essential trace element in the metabolism of all living organisms, including human beings, plants and microorganisms. Low concentrations of permanganate (MnO4 −) can promote fish growth and prevent various fish diseases, but acute exposure to high-level MnO4 − can cause DNA mutations, neurological disorders, excessive liver flow, hallucinations, depression and excessive sleep [22]. As a strong oxidant, MnO4 − is ubiquitously used in wastewater treatment, analytical chemistry, agricultural production, and other fields [23]. The employment of MnO4 − will inevitably produce Mn2+ that contaminate the aquatic environment. Therefore, the development of rapid, convenient, and sensitive approaches is very necessary and crucial for monitoring the trace-level MnO4 − in the waters. Many methodologies for the assay of MnO4 −− have been reported, including titrimetric method [24], colorimetric method [25], spectrophotometry [26], and flame atomic absorption spectrometry [27]. However, these methodologies are often time-consuming, costly, low sensitive, and complex in pretreatment operations, and thus not feasible for routine and rapid analysis. Relatively, fluorescence detection is mostly preferred because of its advantages of simple sample preparation, convenience, speed, high selectivity and sensitivity [28–29].

Herein, the loquat fruit-based CQDs were synthesized by virtue of a facile green hydrothermal methodology, which were subsequently used as an “ON-OFF” probe for the detection of MnO4 −. A series of key parameters influencing the synthetic and analytical efficiency were optimized in detail, including temperature, time, and loquat amount for preparation, as well as solution pH, CQDs amount, reaction temperature and time for detection. Besides, the fluorescence quenching mechanisms of CQDs triggered by MnO4 − were explored by virtue of UV-Vis absorption, fluorescence lifetime, zeta potential, and so on. For the first time, the as-constructed fluorescence probe based on loquat fruit was successfully applied for the assay of trace-level MnO4 − in environmental waters, and it shows great potential applications in conventional monitoring of real-world samples.

Loquat fruits were sourced from a local market in Suzhou, China. All chemical reagents were of analytical grade unless otherwise stated, which were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China): HCl, NaOH, Na2HPO4, KMnO4, AlCl3·6H2O, CoCl2·6H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, AgCl, MnCl2, KCr2O7, Cr2O3, CaCl2, Na2CO3, mercury standard solution and quinine sulfate. All stock and work solutions were prepared using ultrapure water (18.25 MΩ) from Hangzhou Jiejingda Purification System (Hangzhou, China). The water-phase filter membrane (50 mm × 0.45 μm) was acquired from Tianjin Jinteng Experimental Equipment Corporation (Tianjin, China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of fluorescent CQDs from loquat fruits
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs were prepared by employing the fresh loquat fruits as carbon sources through a facile and green hydrothermal method. Prior to synthesis, the fresh loquat fruit was washed thoroughly in deionized water, peeled, cored and ground into slurry. After that, an aliquot of the aforementioned slurry samples was collected and mixed with 35-mL ultrapure water. The mixed solution was transferred into a 200-mL Teflon-lined autoclave and heated at 200 ℃ for 12 h. After heating, the autoclave was cooled to room temperature. Then, the product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and the obtained supernatant was filtered with a 0.45-μm membrane filter. Finally, the resultant product was dialyzed in a dialysis bag (MWCO = 1000 Da) for 24 h, and the CQDs were obtained and stored at 4 ℃ for further use. Scheme 1 vividly elaborates the overall experimental design for the preparation procedures of loquat-based CQDs.

The morphology and size of loquat-based CQDs were investigated with a JEM-2100plus transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL, Japan). Surface electronic state was analyzed by X-ray photoelectron spectrocopy (XPS) on a Thermo Scientific K-Alpha (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were performed by using KBr pellets on a Bruker Tensor Ⅱ FT-IR spectrophotometer (Bruker, Germany). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurement was recorded on a Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffraction (Bruker, Germany). Zeta potential images were obtained using a NANO ZSE Malvern potential analyzer (Malvern, UK). All absorption spectra were recorded on a UV-5500PC UV-Vis absorption spectrophotometer (Shanghai, China). Fluorescence measurements were conducted on a FS-5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh, England). Time-resolved fluorescence spectra were recorded on a FLS1000 photon-counting spectrometer (Edinburgh, England).

Following the footsteps of Zhang and coworkers [15], the QY of as-prepared CQDs was determined based on the comparison with reference substance, quinine sulfate (QY = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4) at the 350-nm excitation wavelength. The following calculation formula is listed as Eq. (1):.
(1)
QY
X
=
QY
ST
×
A
ST
A X
×
I X
I
ST
×
(
η X
η
ST
)
2
where A is the absorbance of samples at 350 nm; I is the integrated emission intensity; and η is the refractive index of the solvent (refractive index η of water = 1.33). The subscripts “X” and “ST” refer to the as-prepared CQDs and quinine sulfate. The absorbance (optical density) of sample (A) were controlled under 0.1 to minimize reabsorption effect [3].

In a typical assay, 50 μL of the loquat-based CQDs was mixed with 2.35 mL of Na2HPO4-citric acid buffer solution (pH = 6, 0.01 M). Subsequently, 100 μL of MnO4 − in the concentration range of 0–250 μM was introduced into the above mixed system. After 3-min incubation at 25℃, the fluorescence intensities (FIs) of mixed solutions were measured at λex/λem = 350/440 nm under both the slit widths of excitation and emission of 3 nm.

To evaluate the anti-interference capacity for the loquat-based CQDs, a series of metal ions were added to the above mixed solutions, including Al3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Cr6+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Cl−, NO3 –, SO4 2− and CO3 2– respectively. The fortification level of each metal ion was set at 150 μM equal to 2-fold that of target anion (MnO4 −).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of MnO4 − in real-world water samples
ce:section.ce:para: Drinking water and tap water were collected from our office and laboratory in Suzhou University of Science and Technology, Suzhou, China. Lake water was taken from the center of Xu Lake, a branch of Taihu Lake, Suzhou, China. Landfill leachate was extracted from Qizishan garbage dump (Suzhou, China). These water samples were firstly centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min, and then filtered with 0.45-μm membrane filters to remove the impurities. For the detection of MnO4 − in real-world water samples, the detailed operational steps followed the description in Section 2.5. The fortified recovery was evaluated at three fortification levels (low (5 μM), medium (20 μM) and high (100 μM)) of MnO4 − in real water samples.

To ensure an excellent performance of the synthesized CQDs, we optimized the important variables in the synthetic reaction, which included the added amount of loquat fruit, reaction time and temperature. When 10 g of loquat fruit was used and reaction time lasted for 12 h, the maximum FI of CQDs was achieved up to ∼3.1×105 a.u. (Fig. S1). As displayed in Fig. S1c, the FIs of CQDs increased monotonically with the reaction temperature from 140 to 220 ℃, but decreased with further increases to 240 ℃. Higher temperature led to decreased FIs of CQDs, which might be explained by occurrence of the oxidation and agglomeration of CQDs under such circumstances [30]. Worth noting, when the temperatures increased from 200 to 220 ℃, no prominent increase in the FIs was observed, which remained nearly constant at 3.02×105 a.u.. As a consequence, 200 ℃ was chosen as the appropriately synthetic temperature in the following trials. Collectively, the optimized hydrothermal reaction conditions were as follows: loquat fruit, 10 g; reaction time, 12 h; and reaction temperature, 200 ℃.

A series of spectral techniques, including TEM, FT-IR, XRD, and XPS, were used to characterize the morphology and size of CQDs. From the TEM image in Fig. 1 a, we could intuitively observe that CQDs displayed nearly sphere and uniform dispersion in aqueous solution without occurrence of apparent aggregation phenomenon. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images exhibited the crystal lattice of CQDs with a lattice spacing of ∼0.21 nm (inset of Fig. 1a). The particle sizes of CQDs spanned the range of 2.56–6.41 nm with the average diameter of 4.46 ± 0.63 nm, which was calculated based on the statistical analysis of 170 particles (Fig. 1b). By XRD, a distinctly broad diffraction peak was observed at 21.89° (2θ), demonstrating the amorphous carbon structure of CQDs (Fig. 1c). Besides, a small absorption peak at 39.41° corresponded to the (100) crystal plane of graphitic carbon [15]. These data evidence that the CQDs had a certain degree of graphitization, which was consistent with the results reported by Zhang and coworkers (2020) [31]. As elaborated in Fig. 1d, the CQDs exhibited the peaks at 3408 and 2972 cm−1, representing the OH and CH stretching vibration. Also, other main peaks were observed for the stretching vibration of CO at 1704 cm−1, CC at 1612 cm−1 and 1292 cm−1, CN at 1084 cm−1, and COO– at 1404 cm−1, respectively. Collectively, these FT-IR data were in general agreement with those described by Wang et al. [32]. The elemental composition and surface chemical bonds for the CQDs were analyzed in detail by XPS. Obviously, the CQDs displayed three typical peaks at 532.06, 399.49, and 285.02 eV (Fig. 2 a), corresponding to O1s, N1s, and C1s, respectively. As revealed by XPS elemental analysis, the CQDs were mainly comprised of oxygen (25.28%), carbon (71.59%) and nitrogen (2.11%). The single-element-fitting XPS energy spectrum was employed to acquire more detailed information on the chemical bond species in the CQDs. The high-resolution spectrum of C1s showed that carbon was related to the formation of four chemical bonds, including CC/CC at 284.7 eV, C-N/C-O at 286.2 eV, OCO at 287.7 eV, and CO at 289.1 eV, respectively (Fig. 2b) [31–33]. In Fig. 2c, the spectrum of O1s could be deconvoluted into two peaks at 531.2 and 532.4 eV, corresponding to CO and COH/COC, respectively [34–35]. The high-resolution N1s spectrum consisted of five peaks at 398.1, 399.1, 400.0, 400.7, and 401.4 eV (Fig. 2d), which were attributable to N in a graphite-like structure, pyridinic-like N, pyrrolic-like N, N-(C)3, and NH, respectively [33,36–37]. These findings provide convincing evidence that both the hydroxyl and carboxylic groups existed in the loquat fruit-based CQDs. Moreover, the zeta potential of CQDs was −27.9 mV (Fig. 4c), hinting the presence of negative charge groups (hydroxyl or carbonyl groups) [12]. The above data lead us to infer that the surface of as-prepared CQDs was functionalized with hydroxyl and carboxylic moieties.

The UV-Vis and fluorescence spectra were adopted to investigate the optical property of CQDs. The maximum absorption peak of CQDs was observed at 265 nm (red curve in Fig. 3 a), which was attributed to π-π* conjugation of CC [12], and the CQDs showed a maximum emission intensity at 440 nm at the 350-nm excitation. A bright blue fluorescence appeared from CQDs under the illumination of 365-nm UV light, while a light yellow color was observed with our naked eyes (inset of Fig. 3a). When the CQDs were excited across the wavelength range of 300–350 nm, their emission wavelength did not change prominently (Fig. 3b). In stark contrast, the emission wavelengths of CQDs were red-shifted when the excitation wavelengths increased from 350 to 420 nm, and also there was a negative relationship between the fluorescence intensities (FIs) and excitation wavelengths. Consequently, the as-fabricated CQDs possessed the excitation-dependent and emission-independent characteristics, which were related to size distribution, surface state and band gap, as well as distribution of emission trap and presence of oxygen-containing group [38]. The optimized excitation/emission peaks (350/440 nm) were also reflected in the 3D fluorescence spectra (Fig. 3c), and the corresponding color coordinate (0.1935, 0.1927) is presented in the Fig. 3d. The relative QY of loquat-based CQDs was estimated to be 10.36% according to the comparison with quinine sulfate (Fig. S2).

To investigate the stability of CQDs, the effects of several important parameters on the fluorescent performance were examined, which included solution pH, NaCl amount, storage time, and UV continuous irradiation time. As depicted in Fig. S3a, the solutions of CQDs showed the relatively stable FIs at ∼7.0×104 (a.u.) with varying solution pH from 3.0 to 9.0. In sharp contrast, the FIs of CQDs dramatically decreased under the over-acid (pH＜3) or over-alkaline (pH＞9) conditions, which were attributable to the protonation/deprotonation and charge transfer of the related functional groups on the surface of CQDs [34]. It is obvious in Fig. S3b that the FIs of CQDs remained nearly unchanged upon addition of high-concentration salt (1.0 M NaCl), demonstrating the stability of CQDs even under high ionic-strength conditions. After a 120-min irradiation, the FIs of CQDs exhibited a slight decrease by <8.0% (Fig. S3c). Moreover, the CQDs presented the stable illumination intensity over a 60-d storage period (Fig. S3d), which could be explained by their smaller particle sizes and electrostatic repulsion [3]. To sum up, these phenomena/data document that the loquat fruit-based CQDs possess an excellent photostability, and can be used as a fluorescent probe for the assay of target molecules or ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental optimization for the assay of MnO4 −
ce:section.ce:para: For the sake of acquiring the best analytical performance for MnO4 −, several influencing factors, including solution pH, CQDs amount, along with reaction temperature and time, were optimized in detail. The MnO4 − ions could trigger the dramatic fluorescence quenching of CQDs, and thus the CQDs-MnO4 − mixed system was feasible for constructing an “ON-OFF” nanoprobe. The quenched efficiency was presented as (F0-F)/F0, where F0 and F are the FIs of CQDs at 440 nm before and after the addition of MnO4 −, respectively. As elaborated in Fig. S4a, the quenched efficiency was almost constant spanning the solution pH range of 2.0–8.0 at other constant conditions (50 μL of CQDs solution, 3 min of reaction time, and reaction temperature at 25℃). The (F0-F)/F0 values were gradually reduced with increasing CQDs dosages from 50 to 300 μL (Fig. S4b). As the reaction time between CQDs and MnO4 − ions increased up to 15 min, the fluorescence quenching efficiency were firstly increased and then decreased, and the maximum quenching reached at ∼3 min (Fig. S4c) and 25 ℃ (Fig. S4d). As a consequence, the overall optimal conditions for the assay of MnO4 − were as follows: solution pH, 6.0; CQDs amount, 50 μL; reaction time, 3 min; and reaction temperature, 25℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitive and selective detection of MnO4 − by the loquat fruit-based CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the selectivity of this “ON-OFF” nanoprobe towards MnO4 −, the fluorescent effects of CQDs were analyzed upon the addition of various metal ions, including Al3+, Co2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, Mg2+, K+, Zn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Ag+, Mn2+, Cr6+, Cr3+, Ca2+, Cl−, NO3 –, SO4 2− and CO3 2– at the fortification level of 150 μM. As vividly elaborated in Fig. S5a, the F/F0 value of CQDs in the presence of MnO4 − were sharply decreased in concomitant with strong color variation (inset of Fig. S5a), whereas those upon the addition of other metal ions varied slightly. The anti-interference ability of this sensing system was also evaluated by studying the impacts of the possible interfering substances, for example, anions (Cl−, NO3 –, SO4 2− and CO3 2–) widely existed in water. As similar as cations, the addition of the above anions led to no obvious changes in the F/F0 value (Fig. S5b), highlighting that they had no significant effect on the quenched efficiency of as-constructed “ON-OFF” sensing system. Therefore, it can be concluded that the as-synthesized CQDs had a high selectivity for the assay of MnO4 − over other cations and anions. The 440-nm FIs gradually decreased with increasing MnO4 − concentrations across the range of 0–250 μM (Fig. S5c), displaying that the blue fluorescence of CQDs was progressively quenched by MnO4 −. An excellent linear relationship was achieved between the fluorescence-quenching efficiency ((F0-F)/F0) and the concentration of MnO4 −. The resultant linear range (LR) was 0.2–150 μM with coefficient of determination (R2) of 0.9968 (Fig. S5d), and the limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 0.06 μM on the basis of 3σ/S (σ is the standard deviation of 12 blank samples, and S is the slope of the linear correlation) [39]. The LOD (0.06 μM) offered by the loquat fruit-based CQDs was much lower than the maximum allowable residue of MnO4 − (1.8 μM) in drinking water according to the World Health Organization (WHO). These results highlight that the as-fabricated CQDs have high specificity, stability, and strong anti-interference ability for MnO4 −, which provide potential feasibility for actual sample detection.

The newly developed “ON-OFF” nanoprobe based on the loquat fruit-based CQDs was employed to detect the trace-level MnO4 − in real-world waters, including drinking water, tap water, lake water and landfill leachate. The collection and pretreatment processes of water samples were detailed in Section 2.7. In the unfortified samples, the concentrations of MnO4 − were detected to be 0.6 and 2.5 μM in the lake water and landfill leachate samples, respectively. At three fortification levels (low, 5 μM; middle, 20 μM; high, 100 μM), the relative recoveries (RRs) for MnO4 − ranged from 96.0% to 106.0% (Table 2), and the relative standard deviations (RSDs) spanned the range of 1.5–2.7%. The experimental precision was evaluated by analyzing the RSDs of the inter-day and intra-day results in six replicates (n = 6). As generalized in Table 1 , the intra-day and inter-day precision was 1.3–2.9% and 1.6–3.4%, respectively. Moreover, the as-constructed “ON-OFF” fluorescence detection was compared with China Standard indirect iodometry method for the detection of MnO4 − in water samples [40]. The detailed operational procedures and photographs for the indirect iodometry method are described and elaborated in Fig. S7. As generalized in Table S2, the indirect iodometry method gave the relative recoveries for MnO4 − across the range of 94.0–112.0% with RSDs of 1.4–4.1%. Obviously, the RRs offered by the newly developed method was comparable or better than those by the indirect iodometry method. Especially, the present method solves a series of disadvantages lying in the indirect iodometry method, such as requirement of a large volume of organic solvent, complex titration procedure and a long operation time. Collectively, these satisfactory performance metrics lead us to conclude that the loquat fruit-based “ON-OFF” nanoprobe has excellent potential in the conventional monitoring of MnO4 − in environmental waters.

In terms of LODs and LRs, Table 2 summarizes the superiority of the loquat fruit-based CQDs when compared to other methods reported previously [24,26,29,41–45]. The LOD (60 nM) offered by the proposed “ON-OFF” nanoprobe is substantially lower than those (2560 nM) by the luminescent coordination polymer CdL-based fluorescent detection [41], two spectrometric methods (392–450 nM) [24,42], and L-cys-MnZns QDs based fluorescence detection (240 nM) [45]. Relatively, it is comparable with the fluorescence detection based on N,S,P-CQDs (50 nM) [29] and PNI-MSPC microgels (25 nM) [44]. Building upon these superior performance indicators, we posit that the as-constructed CQDs probe offers a novel strategy to detect MnO4 − in environmental waters.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quenching mechanisms of CQDs by MnO4 −
ce:section.ce:para:
Fig. 4 a displays the UV-Vis absorption of MnO4 − and the fluorescence excitation/emission spectra of CQDs. Clearly, a large overlap was observed between the absorption spectrum of MnO4 − and the excitation/emission spectra of CQDs. Consequently, MnO4 − could shield the excitation and emission light from CQDs, implying occurrence of the inner filter effect (IFE) between them [28,42]. When comparing the UV-Vis absorption of the CQDs, MnO4 − solution and CQDs + MnO4 − mixed solution, we did not observe new absorption band for the mixed solution (Fig. 4b), which evidenced that no chemical interactions, such as π-π conjugation, appeared between CQDs and MnO4 −. In order to further elaborate the underlying quenching mechanisms, the quenching constant was computed using the Stern-Volmer Eq. (2):.
(2)
F 0
F
=
K
sv
Q
+ 1
where F0 and F are the 440-nm FIs of CQDs in the absence and presence of MnO4 −, Ksv is Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and Q is the concentration of MnO4 −. As shown in Fig. S6a, the plot of F0/F vs. the concentration of MnO4 − (0.2–150 μM) is fit for the linear Stern-Volmer equation. The Ksv for a dynamic quenching mechanism is always in the range of 102-103 [20]. However, the Ksv value of MnO4 − was only 0.02 (Fig. S6b), which was far more less than that of dynamic quenching. As a result, it can be inferred that the quenching mechanism of CQDs by MnO4 − belongs to a static quenching. Fig. 4d shows the fluorescence lifetime decay of CQDs in the presence and absence of MnO4 −. The corresponding lifetimes were determined by fitting the decay curves with the double-exponential function (Fig. S6c and S7d). The average fluorescence lifetimes were computed using the calculation formula is as follows:
(3)
τ =
A 1
τ
1
2
+
A 2
τ
2
2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
Where τ represents the average lifetime of the CQDs; τ1 and τ2 denote the fitted fluorescence lifetime; and A1 and A2 indicate the corresponding proportion. Based on the above Eq. (3), the CQDs was computed to have an average fluorescence lifetime of 3.3729 ns. Upon the addition of MnO4 −, the fluorescence lifetime of CQDs was 3.0670 ns. As listed in Table S1, the slight changes in the fluorescence lifetimes suggested that the quenching by MnO4 − belonged to a static quenching mechanism, which was similar to the previous report by Zhang’s group (2021) [30]. Additionally, the zeta potential of the CQDs solution was −27.9 mV, and that of the mixed solution turned to be −8.7 mV after adding MnO4 − (Fig. 4c). Changes in zeta potentials in the presence/absence of MnO4 − might result from the coordination of MnO4 − with oxygen-rich and amino-rich groups on the surface of CQDs. Inspired by the above analyses, we posit that the interaction mechanisms between CQDs and MnO4 − is the joint action of IFE and static quenching.

In summary, the loquat fruit-based CQDs were fabricated by a facile/green thermal method, which exhibited strongly built-in fluorescence (QY of 10.36%) and bright blue color under UV lamp. They showed spherical shape with an average size of 4.46 nm, and were rich in multi-functional hydrophilic groups, such as –OH, CC, and –COOH, on the surface of CQDs. Based on the prominent quenching phenomenon from MnO4 −, the as-fabricated CQDs were employed to construct an “ON-OFF” nanoprobe, which offered low LOD and satisfactory RR for MnO4 − detection in environmental waters. The MnO4 −-induced quenching mechanisms were confirmed to be the joint action of IFE and static quenching. Compared to an authentic indirect iodometry method and previous approaches, the newly developed loquat-based nanofluoroprobe possesses comparable or even better analytical performance for the assay of trace-level MnO4 −, thereby possessing great application values in conventional monitoring.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was jointly sponsored by theChina National Natural Science Foundation (21876125 and 22076134), Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Environmental Science and Engineering (Zd1802), and Suzhou City Science and Technology Project (SNG2018050).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2022.107374.

ce:section.ce:para:
